inline_review:
commit 33886078a3523a1ec117590de8e9c0d0d2ea03c1
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/stat: monitor all System RAM resources

This commit attempts to monitor all System RAM resources instead of just the
biggest one, which is particularly relevant on NUMA systems. It selects a
physical address range covering all System RAM areas.

> diff --git a/mm/damon/stat.c b/mm/damon/stat.c
> index f9a2028483b0..c5af8ad4bcb6 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/stat.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/stat.c

[ ... ]

> +static int damon_stat_set_monitoring_region(struct damon_target *t,
> +		unsigned long addr_unit, unsigned long min_region_sz)
> +{
> +	struct damon_addr_range addr_range;
> +	struct damon_stat_system_ram_range_walk_arg arg = {};
> +
> +	walk_system_ram_res(0, -1, &arg, damon_stat_system_ram_walk_fn);
> +	if (!arg.walked)
> +		return -EINVAL;
> +	addr_range.start = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.start, addr_unit);
> +	addr_range.end = damon_stat_res_to_core_addr(
> +			arg.res.end + 1, addr_unit);
> +	return damon_set_regions(t, &addr_range, 1, min_region_sz);
> +}

Does this single bounding box incorrectly include unpopulated address gaps
between discrete System RAM resources?

On systems with non-contiguous physical memory, such as NUMA architectures,
there can be massive physical address gaps between memory nodes. By coalescing
all resources into a single addr_range and passing nr_ranges = 1 to
damon_set_regions(), DAMON treats these unpopulated gaps as part of the
monitored memory.

This appears to artificially inflate total_sz in
damon_stat_set_idletime_percentiles(), where the gap could completely
dominate the distribution and skew the percentiles to report that nearly
100% of memory is permanently idle.

Could this also wildly inflate estimated_memory_bandwidth in
damon_stat_set_estimated_memory_bandwidth() if an adaptive DAMON region
bridges valid RAM and a physical gap? The size (r->ar.end - r->ar.start)
would be massively inflated and multiplied by nr_accesses.

Would it be better to collect all discrete System RAM resources into an
array of struct damon_addr_range and pass them to damon_set_regions() using
the actual number of ranges?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"The patch creates a single physical address range spanning all System RAM resources, improperly including unpopulated address gaps (e.g., PCIe holes, NUMA gaps) which severely corrupts DAMON_STAT metrics.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. The patch intends to monitor all System RAM. However, `damon_stat_set_monitoring_region()` walks all RAM resources and creates a single `damon_addr_range` bounding box from the lowest start address to the highest end address, passing it to `damon_set_regions()` with `nr_ranges = 1`.
2. On systems with non-contiguous physical memory (like NUMA architectures), there are often massive physical address gaps (sometimes Terabytes in size) between memory nodes.
3. By coalescing all resources into one bounding box, DAMON treats these unpopulated gaps as part of the monitored memory. While accessing invalid PFNs safely yields zero accesses, the physical sizes of these gaps are still tracked.
4. In `damon_stat_set_idletime_percentiles()`, the total monitored size (`total_sz`) is calculated as the sum of all region sizes. The unpopulated gaps will artificially inflate `total_sz`. On a NUMA system with a large gap, the gap will completely dominate the distribution, skewing the percentiles to report that nearly 100% of memory is permanently idle.
5. In `damon_stat_set_estimated_memory_bandwidth()`, if an adaptive DAMON region bridges valid RAM and a physical gap, its size (`r->ar.end - r->ar.start`) will be massively inflated. Multiplying this inflated size by `nr_accesses` results in wildly inaccurate bandwidth estimations.
6. To properly monitor all System RAM without distortion, the code should collect all discrete System RAM resources into an array of `struct damon_addr_range` and pass them to `damon_set_regions()` using the `nr_ranges` parameter."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 33886078a3523a1ec117590de8e9c0d0d2ea03c1
